title: 95th/99th tail latency - post-call recap and next-step ask
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: James Miller <james.miller@luminahealth.com>, ops@luminahealth.com
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 22 Jun 2028 15:04:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call: 95th/99th tail latency - recap and asks

Thanks for the time today, James — good conversation. Quick recap and the items we need to close so we can scope a short perf validation run against Hosted API (to compare to your incumbent):

Summary of call
- Lumina target envelope: tight end-to-end targets for inference that matter to product (UX): p95 and p99 for the user-facing path.
- Current provider: internal SLA says p95 ~1.0–1.2s, p99 ~2.2–2.6s for 95% of traffic; you saw occasional spikes when concurrency >80.
- Streaming: your flows depend on chunked, partial outputs (progressive render) for long answers (medical summaries).

Asks / next steps we need from Lumina to scope the hosted eval
1) Confirm the target numeric SLOs you want us to validate against (p95/p99 in ms). If you have tiered targets (web vs mobile) call that out.
2) Production-like traffic profile: concurrency target (sustained), expected RPS during peak window, typical input length and output token distribution. Attach sample payloads if possible.
3) Any firewall/VPC restrictions for the private test harness or sample IPs we should expect.
4) A rough window for a 2-hour live injection test next week (Ben/Laura/Rishi will run).

Attached: slo-qualification-checklist.xlsx (internal checklist we use for p95/p99 probes) and call recording.

Goal: run a hosted vs incumbent side-by-side probe that measures end-to-end user-path p95/p99, tail behavior under concurrency, and streaming chunk latency (time-to-first-chunk, inter-chunk spacing).

Thanks — I can turn this into a one-page test plan after we get James' numbers.

—Ben
Ben Carter | Enterprise AE, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0198



From: James Miller <james.miller@luminahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: ops@luminahealth.com
Date: Fri, 22 Jun 2028 18:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call: 95th/99th tail latency - recap and asks

Appreciate the recap, Ben. Some clarification from our side so you can draft the test plan: 

Targets we need validated (these are end-to-end, client-observed):
- Primary (web desktop): p95 <= 1200 ms, p99 <= 2400 ms
- Secondary (mobile): p95 <= 1500 ms, p99 <= 2800 ms

Traffic profile and concurrency
- Peak RPS: ~35 requests/sec sustained for 20–30 minutes during our busiest hour
- Burst concurrency: spikes up to ~120 concurrent active requests (user sessions) for short windows (2–5 minutes)
- Sustained concurrency target for probes: 80 concurrent workers to match production baseline

Streaming requirements
- Responses are streamed in chunks (first chunk = time-to-first-byte for user render). Time-to-first-chunk is important — we want this under 400 ms for 90% of streaming calls. Inter-chunk spacing should not exceed 300 ms on average for long responses (>512 tokens).

Current provider measurement notes
- Our internal telemetry (attached sample logs) show p99 spikes correlate with large input sizes and sustained concurrency above ~90. We can share anonymized traces if that helps.

We are available for a 2-hour window next Wednesday (2028-06-28) between 09:00–12:00 PT for a live run. If you can send a short test plan and sample payloads, we’ll align infra and provide an ops contact for firewall whitelisting.

Thanks,
James
James Miller | Head of Platform Engineering, Lumina Health
james.miller@luminahealth.com



From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>, James Miller <james.miller@luminahealth.com>
Date: Sat, 23 Jun 2028 09:12:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call: 95th/99th tail latency - recap and asks

Thanks James — this is very helpful. Internal action items and proposed probe plan: 

Internal owners
- Laura: test plan author, orchestrate hosted probes, measure time-to-first-chunk and inter-chunk latency metrics.
- Rishi: coordinate streaming instrumentation (we'll add custom headers to measure chunk timings) and run incumbent side measurements if Lumina provides traces.
- Ben: commercial/ops coordination and scheduling.

Proposed short probe (2-hour window) outline
- Warm-up: 15m ramp to sustained 80 workers (to replicate steady-state).
- Measurement window: 60m sustained at 80 workers to record baseline p95/p99 and streaming chunk metrics.
- Burst tests: three 5-minute bursts to 120 concurrency to capture tail amplification.
- Long-response sampling: send 200 requests with >512-token expected outputs to validate inter-chunk spacing.

Requirements from Lumina for the plan to run
- Sample payload set (200 anonymized requests).
- One ops contact and list of IPs to whitelist (we'll use ephemeral hosts but will require outbound/inbound allowances for the probe harness).
- If you want us to run the incumbent side simultaneously, we'll need read access to the anonymized traces or an endpoint that we can hit to compare metrics side-by-side.

I'll author a one-page test plan and post it to the shared Drive link Ben included. Also attaching the internal checklist Ben referenced (slo-qualification-checklist.xlsx) for transparency.

Laura

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>

Date: Fri, 22 Jun 2028 15:04:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call: 95th/99th tail latency - recap and asks

Thanks for the time today, James — good conversation. Quick recap and the items we need to close so we can scope a short perf validation run against Hosted API (to compare to your incumbent):

Summary of call
- Lumina target envelope: tight end-to-end targets for inference that matter to product (UX): p95 and p99 for the user-facing path.
- Current provider: internal SLA says p95 ~1.0–1.2s, p99 ~2.2–2.6s for 95% of traffic; you saw occasional spikes when concurrency >80.
- Streaming: your flows depend on chunked, partial outputs (progressive render) for long answers (medical summaries).

Asks / next steps we need from Lumina to scope the hosted eval
1) Confirm the target numeric SLOs you want us to validate against (p95/p99 in ms). If you have tiered targets (web vs mobile) call that out.
2) Production-like traffic profile: concurrency target (sustained), expected RPS during peak window, typical input length and output token distribution. Attach sample payloads if possible.
3) Any firewall/VPC restrictions for the private test harness or sample IPs we should expect.
4) A rough window for a 2-hour live injection test next week (Ben/Laura/Rishi will run).

Attached: slo-qualification-checklist.xlsx (internal checklist we use for p95/p99 probes) and call recording.

Goal: run a hosted vs incumbent side-by-side probe that measures end-to-end user-path p95/p99, tail behavior under concurrency, and streaming chunk latency (time-to-first-chunk, inter-chunk spacing).

Thanks — I can turn this into a one-page test plan after we get James' numbers.

—Ben
Ben Carter | Enterprise AE, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0198



From: James Miller <james.miller@luminahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: ops@luminahealth.com
Date: Fri, 22 Jun 2028 18:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call: 95th/99th tail latency - recap and asks

Appreciate the recap, Ben. Some clarification from our side so you can draft the test plan: 

Targets we need validated (these are end-to-end, client-observed):
- Primary (web desktop): p95 <= 1200 ms, p99 <= 2400 ms
- Secondary (mobile): p95 <= 1500 ms, p99 <= 2800 ms

Traffic profile and concurrency
- Peak RPS: ~35 requests/sec sustained for 20–30 minutes during our busiest hour
- Burst concurrency: spikes up to ~120 concurrent active requests (user sessions) for short windows (2–5 minutes)
- Sustained concurrency target for probes: 80 concurrent workers to match production baseline

Streaming requirements
- Responses are streamed in chunks (first chunk = time-to-first-byte for user render). Time-to-first-chunk is important — we want this under 400 ms for 90% of streaming calls. Inter-chunk spacing should not exceed 300 ms on average for long responses (>512 tokens).

Current provider measurement notes
- Our internal telemetry (attached sample logs) show p99 spikes correlate with large input sizes and sustained concurrency above ~90. We can share anonymized traces if that helps.

We are available for a 2-hour window next Wednesday (2028-06-28) between 09:00–12:00 PT for a live run. If you can send a short test plan and sample payloads, we’ll align infra and provide an ops contact for firewall whitelisting.

Thanks,
James

title: POC onramp: API + streaming integration checkpoints
preview:
From: Lina Torres <lina.torres@brightbridge.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 21 Jun 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick follow-up from earlier — POC objectives and questions

Thanks again for the demo this morning. Couple quick clarifications from our side as we sketch the trial: 

- Primary success criteria for us are: (1) intent recognition accuracy for support routing (we operate a multi-intent classifier + rerank), (2) 95th p95 generation latency under 300ms for short prompts (<= 256 tokens), and (3) clear observability for per-route token spend.

- We want to test both standard REST calls and streaming for long-response scenarios (agent workflows). Important: tool calling must be isolated to the trial environment; we can provide a staging webhook.

Next actions we need from Redwood to finalize scope: a short POC plan that shows the integration checkpoints (SDK routes, streaming endpoints, tool-call webhook handshake), owners, and a 2-week timeline. Also sharing our system overview (attached).

Lina
Ops Lead, BrightBridge
[Attachment: BrightBridge-Sys-Overview.pdf (application/pdf)]

From: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
To: Lina Torres <lina.torres@brightbridge.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 21 Jun 2028 10:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick follow-up from earlier — POC objectives and questions

Lina — thanks for the clear ask and the system doc. I’ve sketched a concise POC onramp below; will drop a formal sheet by EOD. High-level: 2-week technical trial, focus on API + streaming + tool-calling through a staging webhook.

POC checkpoints (short):
- Week 0 (pre-kick): credentials + network (VPC allowlist for staging IPs), HubSpot kickoff link. Owner: Alex (Redwood) / BrightBridge ops
- Day 1: REST smoke tests (/v1/generate for sync, /v1/embeddings for similarity). Owner: Marissa (SE) / BB dev
- Day 2–4: Streaming integration (/v1/stream) for agent-style conversations + client-side reconnect behavior. Owner: Karthik (infra/stream) / BB dev
- Day 5–7: Tool-calling handshake (staging webhook, signing header) and fail-open fallback test. Owner: Jordan (solutions) / BB ops
- Week 2: Acceptance run and success-matrix evaluation (latency, accuracy, cost). Owner: Alex & Marissa / BB PM

Success criteria draft (for acceptance):
- Intent routing: top-1 intent accuracy >= 86% on the provided eval set
- Streaming: p95 token-delivery latency <= 300ms under trial load (burst 50qps)
- Cost: average per-response token cost within target band (we'll baseline during run)

Deliverables I’ll attach tonight: MS Excel success matrix, curl examples for SDK and streaming, and a small sample Node client for reconnects.

Quick Qs for your engineers: 1) Do you have an allowlist IP range or prefer token- or DNS-based allowlisting? 2) Is the staging webhook reachable behind a static IP?

—Alex
Account Executive, Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 21 Jun 2028 11:28:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick follow-up from earlier — POC objectives and questions

Adding a couple implementation notes for the team and for Lina: 

- SDK routes: recommend shipping with the official Redwood JS SDK (v1.4.0+). Key calls we will surface in examples: 
  * client.generate({ model, prompt, max_tokens, streaming: false }) -> /v1/generate
  * client.stream({ model, prompt }) -> /v1/stream (Server-sent events / chunked transfer)
  * client.callTool({ tool, input, callbackUrl }) -> triggers our tool-calling flow; we will sign callbacks with x-redwood-signature header.

- For streaming reconnects we’ll provide a sample token-refresh approach and an incremental-kv cache header so clients can resume without repeating context where possible.

- Observability: we will enable per-route token counts and attach a short trace id (x-rdwd-trace) to every request for correlating with your telemetry.

I’ll prepare the poc-success-matrix.xlsx with explicit test vectors (intent eval set, latency scenarios, cost thresholds).

Marissa
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Lina Torres <lina.torres@brightbridge.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 22 Jun 2028 08:47:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick follow-up from earlier — POC objectives and questions

Thanks — that looks good and pragmatic. Quick answers to your questions: 
- Allowlist: we can open a /24 on our staging VPC for Redwood staging IPs; we’ll provide IPs by Monday.
- Webhook: staging URL is reachable but sits behind our corporate proxy; we can provide a static egress IP.

A couple of asks from our engineering lead: 
1) We need a signed sample callback and the header verification snippet in Python (our stack is Python + FastAPI).
2) Can you also include a short test harness that runs the streaming scenario with a 50 qps burst and emits p95/p50 metrics to stdout?

If you can get the sample artifacts and success matrix by Monday, we can kick the trial on Tue/Wed next week.

Lina


From: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
To: Lina Torres <lina.torres@brightbridge.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 25 Jun 2028 09:05:00 -07:00
Subject: POC artifacts: success matrix + samples (attached)

Lina — attaching the initial artifacts per our plan. Short notes: 
- poc-success-matrix.xlsx contains test vectors, pass/fail thresholds and owner columns (Redwood/BB). Please review the intent eval set located in the shared Drive link.
- Attached a Python snippet for verifying x-redwood-signature and a small Node-based streaming harness that simulates the 50qps burst and prints p50/p95. The harness uses chunked transfer emulation and reconnect logic.

Next steps: 
- BrightBridge provides IP allowlist + confirmation of staging webhook reachability (tomorrow)
- We finalize kickoff call (30 min) once IPs are confirmed — propose Wed 26 Jun 10:00am PT

Thanks — Alex
[Attachment: poc-success-matrix.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet)]

From: Lina Torres <lina.torres@brightbridge.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 23 Jun 2028 17:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: POC artifacts: success matrix + samples (attached)

Confirmed — our networking team provided the /24 and static egress info (details in the Drive link). Wed 26 Jun 10:00am PT works for the kickoff. We reviewed the matrix and have one small tweak: increase intent accuracy threshold to >=88% for top-1 on the eval set (we have a larger baseline now).

We’ll also attach a short list of 12 edge-case prompts to the Drive folder for the intent eval. Looking forward — Lina

Thanks again for the product walkthrough last week. Procurement and our engineering team reviewed the RFP and we have a few follow-up asks specific to on-prem / VPC-hosted deployments before we can proceed to the security panel: 

- Operator access matrix: roles, minimum required privileges, and separation of duties for on-call operators.

title: Re: Landing criteria and quick alignment — Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private
preview:
From: Emily Turner <emily.turner@novaledger.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Sam Gupta <sam.gupta@novaledger.com>
Date: Mon, 18 Jun 2028 15:22:00 +0000
Subject: Redwood eval — deployment preferences and quick clarifications

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the demo this morning — it was helpful to see the hosted API flow. A few follow-ups from our side as we try to map internal constraints to deployment options:

- Data residency: our legal team wants assurance that PII never leaves the US region for the pilot. Is that guaranteed on Hosted?
- Throughput needs: initial pilot will be ~50 req/sec with occasional bursts to 200 req/sec for short campaigns.
- SLAs: what latency SLOs should we expect for the hosted route vs Dedicated? We care about 95p latency under 250ms if possible.
- Sandbox access: can we get a sandbox endpoint to run our load test before committing to a Dedicated PO?

Also curious about caching (prefix / KV) — will hosted support prefix caching for similar prompts? If not, what's the recommended pattern?

If easier, happy to hop on a 20-min align call later this week.

Best,
Emily
Emily Turner
Product Manager, NovaLedger
---
Attachment: requirements-summary.xlsx (stated, not included)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Turner <emily.turner@novaledger.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 19 Jun 2028 09:08:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Redwood eval — deployment preferences and quick clarifications

Hi Emily — thanks for the clear list. I pulled Marissa (solutions) and Neha (security/compliance) onto this thread so we can cover the different aspects. Quick answers below and a suggested next steps list.

Short answers:
- Data residency: Hosted API can be pinned to US-only regions; we operate per-region tenants and can restrict processing to US data centers for the pilot. For stronger contractual residency or air-gapped needs, Private (VPC/on-prem) is required.
- Throughput: the hosted default endpoints support bursts, but production-grade sustained 200 req/sec is best supported via Dedicated (reserved GPU pool). For your pilot, we recommend a hosted sandbox for functional testing and a short Dedicated proof window if you need consistent throughput.
- Latency SLOs: typical hosted 95p token latency for our curated L2 models is ~120–220ms depending on sequence lengths; Dedicated provides tighter SLOs with committed capacity. We'll share model-specific baselines.
- Caching: hosted supports prefix/KV caching for keys we can identify (prefix hit rates), and we can enable automatic KV caching on the endpoint you test.

Proposed next steps:
1) I’ll provision a US-only sandbox endpoint (hosted) for NovaLedger by Wed EOD — endpoint + API key + basic quota.
2) We’ll attach a short hosted pilot checklist (attached) and suggested traffic profile.
3) If tests show sustained throughput needs, we can brief Dedicated sizing and a 2-week reservation trial.
4) Neha will follow up with the SOC2 / data handling note you’ll need for legal.

I attached a short hosted-pilot-checklist.xlsx with the suggested traffic patterns and what we need from your side for the sandbox.

Can we lock a 20-min provisioning call tomorrow at 10:00am PT?

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
Phone: +1 (415) 555-0132
---
Attachment: hosted-pilot-checklist.xlsx (see attached)

> > (original message quoted)

To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 19 Jun 2028 10:32:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Redwood eval — deployment preferences and quick clarifications

Thanks Ben — quick clarifications before the call:

- Sandbox: for the tests we plan to run, will the sandbox provide the same KV/prefix cache behavior as production hosted endpoints? We want to validate hit rates.
- Burst behavior: you mentioned bursts are supported, do you throttle connections at the endpoint level or queue requests? We want to understand retry/backoff recommendations for our side.
- SLO numbers: could you share the specific model baseline you referenced (token sizes ~64–128)? That will help us set expectations with our infra team.

If tomorrow at 10:00am PT works, I can have Sam (engineering) on the call to go over load profile. Otherwise Thursday morning also OK.

Appreciate the checklist attachment — will review now.

Best,
Emily

> On Tue, Jun 19, 2028 at 09:08 Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai> wrote:
> > (reply quoted)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Turner <emily.turner@novaledger.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 20 Jun 2028 13:10:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Landing criteria and quick alignment — Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private

Hi Emily — quick follow-up with the concrete artifacts so your infra and legal teams can move forward.

1) Sandbox provisioning
- Endpoint: https://sandbox.redwood.ai/api/v1/echo
- Region: US-WEST (processing restricted to US datacenters for this key)
- Quota: 100k tokens/day, burst to 200 req/sec for 10 minutes (we’ll enable a temporary burst window for tests)
- Behavior: sandbox mirrors hosted endpoint behavior including KV/prefix caching, but performance is not guaranteed for production SLOs — it’s for functional and load validation.

2) Baseline SLOs (example for curated L2 model, 64–128 tokens):
- Hosted (US region): 95p token latency: ~150–210ms
- Dedicated (sized): 95p token latency: ~110–160ms (with capacity reservation)

3) Burst and throttling guidance
- Hosted endpoints allow short-term bursts; sustained high throughput should go to Dedicated.
- If requests exceed quota we return 429 with Retry-After; recommended client strategy: exponential backoff with jitter and Circuit Breaker for sustained 10s windows.

4) Compliance & data handling
- Neha: attaching a short note for legal re: US-only processing and log retention. She’ll follow with SOC2 artifact links.

5) Next actions
- Ben: Provision sandbox (done) and share keys via secure link (separate email).
- Emily: Validate KV hit rates and run traffic profile; share results by next Tue.
- Marissa: Prepare Dedicated sizing options if you show steady >100 req/sec sustained for 24+ hours.

Links: HubSpot deal: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/12345/deal/67890
DocuSign (placeholder for MSA): https://docusign.net/link/abc123

If that looks good I’ll send the sandbox credentials over the secure delivery channel and we’ll lock a short session with Sam to walk through test scenarios.

Thanks — talk soon.
Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai
Phone: +1 (415) 555-0132

Hi Emily — thanks for the clear list. I pulled Marissa (solutions) and Neha (security/compliance) onto this thread so we can cover the different aspects. Quick answers below and a suggested next steps list.

Short answers:
- Data residency: Hosted API can be pinned to US-only regions; we operate per-region tenants and can restrict processing to US data centers for the pilot. For stronger contractual residency or air-gapped needs, Private (VPC/on-prem) is required.
- Throughput: the hosted default endpoints support bursts, but production-grade sustained 200 req/sec is best supported via Dedicated (reserved GPU pool). For your pilot, we recommend a hosted sandbox for functional testing and a short Dedicated proof window if you need consistent throughput.
- Latency SLOs: typical hosted 95p token latency for our curated L2 models is ~120–220ms depending on sequence lengths; Dedicated provides tighter SLOs with committed capacity. We'll share model-specific baselines.
- Caching: hosted supports prefix/KV caching for keys we can identify (prefix hit rates), and we can enable automatic KV caching on the endpoint you test.

Proposed next steps:
1) I’ll provision a US-only sandbox endpoint (hosted) for NovaLedger by Wed EOD — endpoint + API key + basic quota.

title: Request: Custom uptime addendum & order form language for NovaMed
preview:
From: Elaine Johnson <elaine.johnson@novamed.com>
To: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, procurement@novamed.com
Date: 2028-06-15T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Custom SLA addendum request — uptime & latency commitments

Grace —

Thanks for the demo last week. Procurement asked me to follow up with a specific ask from our reliability team: NovaMed requires a written addendum to the MSA/SOW that includes a 99.99% uptime commitment for the production model endpoints used for clinical workflows, plus a p95 latency SLA of 200ms for single-token generation on typical 2–4k token prompts.

We understand that this is outside standard terms; the main things we need to evaluate are:
- Exact credit schedule for misses at 99.99 / 99.95 / 99.9 tiers
- Evidence that you have multi-region failover and an ops runbook (on-call, RTO targets)
- A short list of mitigations (caching, burst capacity, fallback model variants) you will guarantee for these endpoints

Can you provide (1) a proposed addendum draft we can feed to procurement/legal, (2) any ops evidence or an evidence matrix, and (3) the preferred process for ordering this as an order form amendment (do you prefer redlines to your standard order form or a separate exhibit)? We also need to move quickly: procurement wants the legal language in the next 7 business days.

Happy to hop on a call if that helps — available Thu/Fri PST.

Thanks,
Elaine Johnson
SVP Product, NovaMed
elaine.johnson@novamed.com

[attachments: reliability_requirements_novamed.pdf]

From: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
To: Elaine Johnson <elaine.johnson@novamed.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-06-15T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Custom SLA addendum request — uptime & latency commitments

Elaine —

Appreciate the clear asks. We're able to support a scoped, contract-level addendum for production endpoints, but the exact terms depend on three things: traffic profile, capacity reservation, and whether NovaMed wants dedicated or shared pools for the covered endpoints.

Immediate next steps I propose:
1) I attached a draft "Redwood_Uptime_Addendum_v1.pdf" with an initial credit schedule and proposed wording for the uptime and latency commitments.
2) We can provide an "evidence matrix" (ops diagrams, runbook excerpts, multi-region topology) — see attached evidence_matrix_novamed.xlsx. Our SRE will redact sensitive internal IPs but include the failover and RTO playbook.
3) For the order form, our standard path is redlines to the order form with an attached addendum exhibit. That keeps billing and term references intact. We included a suggested order_form_redlines_novamed.docx.

High level on commitments: we can offer a 99.95% baseline for shared pools with a 4-hour RTO for region failover. For 99.99% we recommend a dedicated capacity reservation (or cross-region reserved capacity) — that lets us commit a stricter credit schedule. Latency guarantees for p95=200ms are achievable under dedicated capacity for the 2–4k prompt profile; for shared pools we would propose a higher p95 threshold. The attached draft shows both options side-by-side.

Soojin, can legal priority-review the draft and indicate which clauses require additional negotiation? David, can SRE validate the RTO language and confirm what can be included in a customer-facing evidence matrix? Ben — can you confirm the commercial uplift for reserved capacity so I can feed that to procurement?

I'll aim to have a revised draft back to you in 48 hours once legal + SRE provide inputs, and we can set up a 30-minute sync if you prefer to walk through the redlines.

Best,
Grace O'Connor
Head of Customer Success — Enterprise
Redwood Inference
grace.oconnor@redwood.com

[attachments: Redwood_Uptime_Addendum_v1.pdf; order_form_redlines_novamed.docx; evidence_matrix_novamed.xlsx]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-06-15T12:07:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Custom SLA addendum request — pricing/credits (internal)

Quick note on credit math and commercial ask:
- For 99.95% baseline (shared): propose 10% service credit for a monthly breach, 25% for consecutive breaches >1 week. No price uplift.
- For 99.99% (dedicated/reserved): propose a 15% monthly credit for single breach, 50% cap if system-wide incident >24h. Requires ~25% uplift on committed spend to reserve capacity.
- For latency guarantees (p95): include a lower-tier SLA (p95 < 350ms) for shared with no credit, and the 200ms p95 with a smaller credit band for dedicated.

I recommend we present both options to NovaMed: 1) faster path (dedicated + uplift) to hit 99.99/200ms; 2) shared with looser SLAs but quicker onboarding.

Ben

From: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
To: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-06-16T09:20:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Custom SLA addendum request — legal review notes (internal)

Grace/Ben —

Reviewed the draft addendum. High level comments:
- Add explicit scope language: define "covered endpoints" by route/model version and environment (prod only). Avoid open-ended coverage.
- Evidence: we can provide a redacted incident history summary and the ops runbook excerpt, but we must not expose internal IPs, third-party vendor contracts, or detailed topology diagrams. We'll include a sanitized topology with failover steps.
- Credits: cap total monthly credits at 100% of monthly recurring charges (MRC) and add an annual aggregation clause so multiple small breaches don't compound unexpectedly.
- Data retention / logs: NovaMed asked for audit logs — propose a 90-day export window for detailed trace logs (longer retention on request as a paid add-on). Put this in the evidence exhibit.
- Order form language: change "Availability" section to reference the addendum exhibit and include an explicit escalation path and contact (SRE escalation contact and legal CLA).

I'll redline the attached order_form_redlines_novamed.docx and return before EOD tomorrow. Also recommend we insist on a defined capacity reservation line item if they want 99.99.

Soojin

HubSpot deal: https://app.hubspot.com/deals/1234567

title: Pilot sizing & deployment economics clarification
preview:
From: Elena Morales <elena@brightcrafted.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 14 Jun 2028 08:23:00 -0700
Subject: Follow-up: deployment path and cost model questions

Hi Aisha — thanks for the productive demo earlier today. Quick follow-up from our side as we start to map internal approvals and the procurement ask.

Recap of our priorities: low 100–200ms tail latency for user-facing chat, data residency in US, and predictable monthly cost for budgeting. We are evaluating three options you outlined — Hosted API, Dedicated capacity (reserved GPU pool), and Private VPC/on-prem for the sensitive subset.

To get us to an internal cost-committee meeting next week, can you confirm: 1) what inputs Redwood needs to produce an apples-to-apples cost worksheet; 2) a simple threshold (monthly tokens or QPS) where Dedicated becomes demonstrably cheaper than per-token Hosted; and 3) any minimum commitment term for Dedicated pilot pricing?

Rough usage signals from our telemetry (this is an early estimate): average 1.2M user messages/month, avg prompt+response = 350 tokens, peak concurrent users ~120 (p95 steady-state QPS ~3.5), expect growth 20% q/q if demo looks good. A small subset (~7%) will require the private-data workflow.

If you can get a short cost sketch and next steps by EOD Monday (US), that helps our budget owner. Also include any legal/PO triggers for starting a Dedicated pilot.

Thanks —
Elena Morales
Head of Product
BrightCrafted AI



From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena@brightcrafted.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 14 Jun 2028 10:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Follow-up: deployment path and cost model questions

Hi Elena — great to meet you today, and thanks for the clear priorities. Below is a concise plan and the inputs we used to build the attached cost workbook (redwood_pilot_pricing_workbook.xlsx). I kept the assumptions conservative; if you can confirm the usage numbers you quoted we’ll tighten the math.

1) Inputs we need from you to finalize a precise worksheet:
- Avg tokens per request (you provided 350 — please confirm);
- Expected monthly active requests (1.2M messages × usage multiplier if bot-initiated follow-ups count);
- Peak sustained QPS and p99 latency SLO;
- Percentage of traffic requiring private-data handling (you said ~7%);
- Desired SLA/uptime and whether burst capacity is acceptable.

2) Quick threshold guidance (ballpark):
- Hosted (per-token): best for proof-of-concept and low-to-medium volume with unpredictable bursts. Example ballpark math: if Hosted list price = X per 1k tokens, with your numbers -> ~420M tokens/month (1.2M messages × 350 tokens) => hosted_cost ≈ (420,000k tokens / 1k) * X.
- Dedicated (reserved capacity): starts to make sense when sustained throughput requires a standing GPU pool (rough proxy: when sustained qps and p50 token rate push you into continuous GPU usage for >10–14 hours/day). For many customers we see crossover at ~10–20M tokens/month depending on model size and microarchitecture. For your profile (420M tokens/month is significantly above that), Dedicated will likely lower unit cost once we size the pool correctly.
- Private VPC/on-prem: recommended when data residency, audit logs, or strict network isolation are non-negotiable. It carries additional setup and infra costs (network egress, customer-managed KMS/HSM) and typically requires a longer onboarding window.

3) Commitment/terms for Dedicated pilot:
- We can do a 3-month pilot with a discounted committed pool (30–50% discount off committed pricing) to validate performance/cost; requires a simple MSA addendum and a purchase order for the first month. If procurement needs a shorter commercial instrument we can do a trial credit on Hosted while we finalize Dedicated terms.

Attached is a starter workbook with two tabs: Hosted-vs-Reserved (uses your quoted token numbers) and Dedicated-sizing (maps GPU counts to expected sustained tokens/QPS). From: Elena Morales <elena@brightcrafted.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 14 Jun 2028 08:23:00 -0700
Subject: Follow-up: deployment path and cost model questions

Hi Aisha — thanks for the productive demo earlier today. Quick follow-up from our side as we start to map internal approvals and the procurement ask.

Recap of our priorities: low 100–200ms tail latency for user-facing chat, data residency in US, and predictable monthly cost for budgeting. We are evaluating three options you outlined — Hosted API, Dedicated capacity (reserved GPU pool), and Private VPC/on-prem for the sensitive subset.

To get us to an internal cost-committee meeting next week, can you confirm: 1) what inputs Redwood needs to produce an apples-to-apples cost worksheet; 2) a simple threshold (monthly tokens or QPS) where Dedicated becomes demonstrably cheaper than per-token Hosted; and 3) any minimum commitment term for Dedicated pilot pricing?

Rough usage signals from our telemetry (this is an early estimate): average 1.2M user messages/month, avg prompt+response = 350 tokens, peak concurrent users ~120 (p95 steady-state QPS ~3.5), expect growth 20% q/q if demo looks good. A small subset (~7%) will require the private-data workflow.

If you can get a short cost sketch and next steps by EOD Monday (US), that helps our budget owner. Also include any legal/PO triggers for starting a Dedicated pilot.

Thanks —
Elena Morales
Head of Product
BrightCrafted AI



From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena@brightcrafted.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 14 Jun 2028 10:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Follow-up: deployment path and cost model questions

Hi Elena — great to meet you today, and thanks for the clear priorities. Below is a concise plan and the inputs we used to build the attached cost workbook (redwood_pilot_pricing_workbook.xlsx). I kept the assumptions conservative; if you can confirm the usage numbers you quoted we’ll tighten the math.

1) Inputs we need from you to finalize a precise worksheet:
- Avg tokens per request (you provided 350 — please confirm);
- Expected monthly active requests (1.2M messages × usage multiplier if bot-initiated follow-ups count);
- Peak sustained QPS and p99 latency SLO;
- Percentage of traffic requiring private-data handling (you said ~7%);
- Desired SLA/uptime and whether burst capacity is acceptable.

2) Quick threshold guidance (ballpark):
- Hosted (per-token): best for proof-of-concept and low-to-medium volume with unpredictable bursts. Example ballpark math: if Hosted list price = X per 1k tokens, with your numbers -> ~420M tokens/month (1.2M messages × 350 tokens) => hosted_cost ≈ (420,000k tokens / 1k) * X.
- Dedicated (reserved capacity): starts to make sense when sustained throughput requires a standing GPU pool (rough proxy: when sustained qps and p50 token rate push you into continuous GPU usage for >10–14 hours/day). For many customers we see crossover at ~10–20M tokens/month depending on model size and microarchitecture. For your profile (420M tokens/month is significantly above that), Dedicated will likely lower unit cost once we size the pool correctly.
- Private VPC/on-prem: recommended when data residency, audit logs, or strict network isolation are non-negotiable. It carries additional setup and infra costs (network egress, customer-managed KMS/HSM) and typically requires a longer onboarding window.

3) Commitment/terms for Dedicated pilot:
- We can do a 3-month pilot with a discounted committed pool (30–50% discount off committed pricing) to validate performance/cost; requires a simple MSA addendum and a purchase order for the first month. If procurement needs a shorter commercial instrument we can do a trial credit on Hosted while we finalize Dedicated terms.

Attached is a starter workbook with two tabs: Hosted-vs-Reserved (uses your quoted token numbers) and Dedicated-sizing (maps GPU counts to expected sustained tokens/QPS). The workbook lists assumed model family and quantization that drive GPU throughput — we’ll refine after Vivek reviews your latency/throughput goals.

Next steps I propose:
- You confirm the token averages and whether the 1.2M messages is counting user-only messages or includes system-initiated messages.
- I’ll book a 30-min call between your infra owner + Vivek to review latency and sizing details (Vivek can propose the recommended Dedicated SKU/throughput).
- Ben will share the pilot commercial outline and the mutual action plan doc.

If that works I’ll send the calendar invite and update the workbook based on any clarifications.

Best —

title: POC inputs onboarding: prompt/log delivery & owners
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Liam Patel <liam.patel@nimbus.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-06-12T15:10:00-07:00
Subject: POC inputs onboarding: prompt/log delivery & owners

Hi Liam —

Great to meet earlier. Quick recap and concrete next steps so we stay aligned for the 2-week POC: 

Call recap / goals:
- Objective: validate Redwood inference latency/cost on typical Nimbus prompt workload and evaluate answer quality against your existing rule-based assistant.
- Success criteria (proposed):
  * Mean latency (p95) under 250ms for single-turn prompts at 8k token windows,
  * Accuracy uplift >= 15% on labeled eval set (we'll use your golden labels),
  * No PII leakage detected in automated checks; any flagged items must be remediated.

Files we need from your side (by EOD Thu, 2028-06-15):
- Prompt set: CSV with columns (prompt_id, prompt_text, context, expected_output_label) — ~10k rows ideal.
- Raw logs: anonymized request logs (timestamp, user_id_hash, prompt_text, model_response) — if you must, replace user_id with hashed id; we can accept presigned S3 or SFTP.
- A small labeled eval CSV (200-500 examples) to use as gold standard for initial quality checks.

Ownership (please confirm):
- Data transfer owner for Nimbus: Liam (you) — confirm whether security needs a DPA or approved S3 bucket.
- Redwood owners: Marissa Cole will lead evaluation and result reporting; Priyom Das will own data ingestion and redaction tooling.

Operational checklist (we'll complete on our side):
- Provide sample ingestion script and schema.
- Run automated PII redaction pass on your raw logs (we can do this if needed).
- Share HubSpot deal + any procurement contact if you need a PO for trial credits.

Attachments: sample CSV template attached for prompt set (prompt_sample_template.csv).

Please reply with preferred transfer method (presigned S3, secure Dropbox, or SFTP) and confirm owners/timeline. Happy to jump on a 15-min call if helpful.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Solutions, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0124

- Files: we'll deliver a prompt CSV (~12k rows) + a zipped log export (redacted) and a 300-example labeled eval set.

Subject: Proposed MSA redlines — on‑prem addendum & operator custody

Ben,

Attached are our initial redlines to the MSA and a spreadsheet capturing security requirements for an on‑prem/VPC deployment. Key asks are:

- Explicit language allowing a sealed snapshot handoff to an independent custodian (we've labeled this "escrow-lite")
- Defined operator access model (witnessed snapshots vs. remote keys)
- A 30‑day audit window with scope for evidence delivery and forensic snapshots
- Fall‑back / termination pathway if Redwood ceases operations

Files attached:
- Acme_Redlines_MSA_20280613.docx (redline)
- Exhibit-OnPrem-Security.xlsx (requirements checklist)

Procurement would like to understand whether Redwood can accept custody-lite with a third‑party custodian and provide attestation, or whether customer keys would be required.

Our preferred timeline: procurement needs a mark‑up response by EOD Friday 6/17 to keep our internal cycle on track. Can you confirm availability for a short call tomorrow afternoon to triage?

Thanks,

--

Sr. Counsel, Acme HealthTech

Lydia —

Thanks for the redlines and the checklist. Quick triage below; Marissa and David are looped for legal detail and Rachel will help on the ops/security constraints.

High‑level answers:
- Escrow‑lite: Redwood can support a sealed snapshot handoff to an independent custodian (no direct customer key handoff). We typically provide a notarized snapshot with a witness protocol and documented access procedure.
- Operator access: we can commit to "witnessed snapshot" access only (no remote keys for routine ops). We can provide an operator attestation and a short runbook for witness sessions.
- Audit window: 30 days' notice is acceptable; we'll need to confirm evidence delivery SLAs and any live access conditions.
- Vendor insolvency: we propose a three‑party mechanism — sealed snapshot + custodian with release conditions + court‑enforceable attestation clause.

Lydia / Ben,

Thanks — attaching a draft of suggested clause language and an operator access exhibit for your review.

Key clauses (summary):

1) Escrow‑lite / Sealed Snapshot (draft snippet):
"Upon written notice and subject to the Release Conditions below, Redwood will deposit a sealed, encrypted runtime snapshot with an independent custodian (Custodian). Release of the snapshot to Customer shall occur only upon (a) material breach and failure to cure; (b) bankruptcy or cessation of operations; or (c) written mutual agreement. The Custodian shall not have the ability to unseal or use the snapshot for any purpose other than to deliver it to Customer or its designated agents pursuant to this Section. Redwood will provide an attestation of integrity and operational runbook to the Custodian." 

2) Operator Access Exhibit (summary):
- Operator sessions limited to witnessed snapshot generation and emergency access with dual‑authorization
- All sessions recorded; logs retained for 12 months; attestation signed by operator and independent witness
- No remote key escrow to Redwood for routine operations

3) Evidence / Audit (summary):
- 30‑day advance notice for a forensic audit; Redwood will produce evidence as enumerated in Exhibit A within the agreed SLAs; third‑party forensic review allowed under NDA.

I attached two files for your convenience:
- redwood-escrow-clause-draft.txt (clause language)
- operator-access-exhibit-draft.docx (technical exhibit)

title: Finexa P1: sudden 429s and latency spike — suspected token-bucket / burst policy issue
preview:
From: Sasha Green <sasha.green@finexa.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-06-14 10:12:00 -0700
Subject: High 429 rate + latency spike impacting Finexa payment flows

Alex / Laura —

We started seeing a sharp uptick in 429s and end-to-end latency across our payment validation flows at ~10:05am PT. Error rate jumped from ~0.1% to ~8% and tail latency moved from 120ms -> 1.2s. Customers are seeing failed authorizations.

Attached is a screenshot of the dashboard and a sample request/response showing 429: rate_limit and the token-bucket header showing \"dropped=1\". This looks like throttling on your side — can you confirm whether any quota or burst policy changed?

This is P1 for us; we need an update within 30 minutes.

Thanks,
Sasha Green
Sr. Platform Ops, Finexa
(sasha.green@finexa.com)

Attachment: screenshot_429s.png


From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Sasha Green <sasha.green@finexa.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-06-14 10:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: High 429 rate + latency spike impacting Finexa payment flows

Sasha — thanks for the ping and the screenshot. We see the same spike on our side (matching traces). I’m looping in Marcus to triage the policy/config side and Rachel/Laura to coordinate with your ops and procurement if we need to provide immediate relief (temporary quota bump / credits).

Immediate plan:
- Marcus: can you confirm whether a policy rollout or a default quota change hit Finexa’s org id around 10:00–10:10?
- If this is a burst-policy/token-bucket misconfiguration, propose an emergency rollback to the previous policy for Finexa and disable strict burst enforcement for 30 mins while we stabilize.
- Rachel/Laura: please prepare a customer-facing note and let Sasha know if we need to open a credits/commit exception.

I’ll keep Sasha updated every 15 minutes until we’re stable.

— Alex
Alex Martinez
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
alex.martinez@redwood.ai


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-06-14 10:35:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: High 429 rate + latency spike impacting Finexa payment flows

On Fri, Jun 14, 2028 at 10:22 AM Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Marcus: can you confirm whether a policy rollout or a default quota change hit Finexa’s org id around 10:00–10:10?

I pulled the policy audit and traffic traces. Root cause summary: during the 09:55 global quota-policy push we accidentally applied a new token-bucket profile that set \"burst_capacity\"=0 for a cohort which includes Finexa (policy selector mismatch). The token-bucket algorithm then rejected burst tokens instead of allowing short spikes, which manifests as 429s and raised latency due to retries.

Evidence: SUP-9876 shows the rollout diff and the applied selector. I’m attaching a perf snapshot and a short log snippet.

Mitigation options (fastest first):
1) Emergency rollback of the policy version for Finexa org id (hotfix): expected recovery within ~3–8 minutes after rollback.
2) If rollback risks other customers, temporarily set burst_capacity=2000 for Finexa via an override (takes ~2–5 minutes to apply).
3) Longer term: add a rollout guard that prevents zero-burst policies from targeting non-test orgs.

I recommend immediate rollback. If I get approval I’ll push the rollback and monitor.

— Marcus
Marcus Lin
Senior Software Engineer, Serving Runtime
marcus.lin@redwood.ai

Attachments: perf_snapshot_20280614.json
Related: https://jira.redwood.ai/browse/SUP-9876


From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-06-14 11:02:00 -07:00
Subject: URGENT: Exec attention needed — Finexa P1 impacting production (requesting rollback + credit exception)

Marissa — looping you in because Finexa is a strategic account (DEAL-5561). Impact: authorizations failing for live customers, error rate ~8%, multiple incidents in their support channels. Marcus has identified a policy rollout that set burst_capacity=0 for their cohort; rollback is low risk and should restore service in minutes.

Asks:
- Approve emergency rollback for Finexa org and 30-minute disable of burst enforcement (Marcus to execute).
- Approve a temporary one-time 10% credit for the window if Finexa requests it (Rachel to coordinate legal/billing).
- Notify Execs if Finexa requests a formal SLA credit or escalation.

If approved I’ll proceed immediately and confirm when the rollback completes.

— Alex


From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Sasha Green <sasha.green@finexa.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-06-14 15:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: High 429 rate + latency spike impacting Finexa payment flows

Sasha — update: we performed the emergency rollback at 15:12 PT and removed the strict burst enforcement for Finexa for a 45-minute window. Post-rollback metrics: 429 rate returned to baseline (~0.15%) within ~5 minutes; p95 latency back to ~130ms. We’ve attached the policy_rollback.log and a short note describing the changes.

Evidence: SUP-9876 shows the rollout diff and the applied selector. I’m attaching a perf snapshot and a short log snippet.

Mitigation options (fastest first):
1) Emergency rollback of the policy version for Finexa org id (hotfix): expected recovery within ~3–8 minutes after rollback.
2) If rollback risks other customers, temporarily set burst_capacity=2000 for Finexa via an override (takes ~2–5 minutes to apply).
3) Longer term: add a rollout guard that prevents zero-burst policies from targeting non-test orgs.

I recommend immediate rollback. If I get approval I’ll push the rollback and monitor.

— Marcus
Marcus Lin
Senior Software Engineer, Serving Runtime
marcus.lin@redwood.ai

Attachments: perf_snapshot_20280614.json

title: Chorus security questionnaire — geography, retention, and purge windows (hosted API)
preview:
From: Liam O'Neill <liam.oneill@chorus.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.ai>
Cc: security@chorus.ai
Date: 2028-06-10T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Chorus: hosted API security questionnaire — data geography & retention

Hi Camila,

Thanks for making time last week. Attached is the small security questionnaire our compliance team put together focused on data geography, retention, and erase/backup windows for hosted APIs. We have three quick asks up front:

1) Which physical regions can we guarantee for customer request data and associated logs?
2) How long do request logs, user prompts, and model-level ephemeral caches persist by default?
3) What are your deletion/propagation timelines when a customer requests full data erasure?

We've attached the questionnaire (chorus-questionnaire.pdf) with a handful of checkbox items — if you can return the redwood-specific section, that would be perfect.

Attachment: chorus-questionnaire.pdf (application/pdf)

Appreciate it — if it's easier to walk through over a brief call I'm happy to schedule.

Best,
Liam
Security Operations, Chorus.ai



From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.ai>
To: Liam O'Neill <liam.oneill@chorus.ai>
Cc: security@chorus.ai
Date: 2028-06-10T12:30:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Chorus: hosted API security questionnaire — data geography & retention

Hi Liam — thanks for sending this over. Please find our concise responses below and the redwood-specific section attached as requested (chorus-questionnaire-redwood-section.pdf). I also attached a lightweight data flow diagram and our SOC2 summary for context.

Quick summary (hosted API):
- Region guarantees: Customers may select a region at provisioning; we support US (us-west-2, us-east-1) and EU (eu-west-1) regions for request data and logs. For Chorus we can lock provisioning to US-only. Region selection is enforced at the tenant level and prevents cross-region replication of request data.

- Request logs & prompts retention: By default, request logs and prompt/response bodies are retained for 30 days. Customers can request custom retention windows down to 0 days (ephemeral) via a contract addendum; minimum configurable retention for hosted tenants is 0 days for prompts and 7 days for anonymized usage aggregates.

- Ephemeral/model caches: KV/prefix caches used to accelerate inference are in-memory only and are not persisted to disk. These caches are evicted in a least-recently-used pattern; eviction timing depends on workload but we do not retain cache contents beyond process lifetime.

- Deletion & propagation: When a tenant requests a full erasure (API call or support ticket), we run a coordinated workflow: delete primary records -> invalidate caches -> remove transient logs -> schedule snapshot purge. In practice, deletion of primary request records and logs is immediate (minutes to an hour), while snapshot/backups follow the backup lifecycle (see below). We provide attestations of deletion completion.

- Backups & snapshots: Nightly snapshots of storage are retained for 90 days by default. Snapshots are mechanically immutable; upon a deletion request we mark items for purge and they are removed from subsequent snapshots once the retention window elapses. For stricter needs we offer shorter snapshot retention on Dedicated or via contract terms.

- Audit logs: Administrative audit trails are retained 365 days; they record access to tenant objects and deletion workflows. These logs are accessible to customers through the Console and can also be exported securely.

- Data flows & third parties: Customer request data is encrypted at rest and in transit. We use cloud KMS for key management; we can integrate with customer-managed keys for Private/Dedicated deployments. We do not send raw request data to third-party analytics vendors. Metrics are exported as anonymized aggregates by default.

Attachments: chorus-questionnaire-redwood-section.pdf (application/pdf), redwood-hosted-data-flow-diagram.png (image/png), soc2-summary.pdf (application/pdf)

I've included the redwood section as answers to your PDF checkboxes and added clarifying notes in the flow diagram. If you want, Neha (cc'd) can provide the SOC2 artifact IDs.

Best,
Camila Reyes
Senior Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference
camila.reyes@redwood.ai

On Jun 10, 2028, at 09:12 AM, Liam O'Neill <liam.oneill@chorus.ai> wrote:
> Hi Camila,
> 
> Thanks for making time last week. Attached is the small security questionnaire...

To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-06-11T10:04:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Chorus: hosted API security questionnaire — data geography & retention

Looping you both in — Chorus is asking for more detail on backup/snapshot purge behavior and whether we can offer a shorter snapshot retention for them on hosted. I attached the replies I sent to Liam.

Specific asks from Chorus:
- Can we reduce nightly snapshot retention from 90 days to 30 days for a hosted tenant under a contractual commitment?
- Is the deletion workflow auditable to the level that we can provide a time-stamped deletion attestation?

Notes / items for tradeoffs:
- Shortening snapshot retention increases operational exposure for recovery (we still keep live backups for the active retention window).
- We can provide time-stamped deletion attestations via the audit-log export; the SOC2 artifact includes the internal audit schema (see soc2-summary.pdf).

Neha — can you confirm whether we have any dependencies that would prevent a 30-day snapshot TTL for a particular tenant? Ben — if we can offer 30 days, do we want to surface any price uplift or SLA caveats?

Thanks,
Camila

title: Urgent: SLO carveout & traffic-shift request — model phi7 tail latency
preview:
From: Nimbus Health Ops <ops@nimbushealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-06-11T08:12:00-07:00
Subject: Re: sudden tail latency spike on phi7 (prod)

Ben —

We started seeing a sustained increase in tail latency for our /generate endpoint after the phi7 rollout reached ~60% traffic at 02:00 PT. p95 has gone from ~180ms to 1.8s and p99 is spiking above 3s. This started around 02:10 and has persisted through the morning.

Attached: phi7-p95-graph.png (observability snapshot from our side).

This is impacting a critical customer workflow (scheduling confirmations). Can Redwood please advise: is there a safe fallback variant you can switch us to, or can you revert the rollout? We need a short ETR — also, will this impact our SLA?

Thanks —
Tim Zhao
Ops Lead, Nimbus Health
ops@nimbushealth.com

On Fri, Jun 11, 2028 at 08:05 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Tim — thanks for the alert, I’m pulling in the runtime and product teams now.
> > Ben


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Tim Zhao <tim.zhao@nimbushealth.com>
Date: 2028-06-11T08:28:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: sudden tail latency spike on phi7 (prod) — request for SLO carveout & traffic shift

Team — forwarding an ops escalation from Nimbus Health. Key points:
- Model: phi7 (rolled to 60% at 02:00 PT)
- Symptom: p95 ~1.8s (baseline ~180ms), p99 >3s, sustained ~6 hours
- Customer impact: core scheduling flow is failing during spikes

Asking for two fast decisions:
1) Authorize an immediate traffic shift for Nimbus to a known-stable variant (phi6) while we investigate. We should limit to their environment/route only — not global rollback.
2) Approve a temporary SLO carveout (formal exception) while we root-cause (expected 24-48h window); we need to confirm whether credits apply and what language to share.

Rishi — can runtime confirm whether phi6 is compatible and estimate RTO for a targeted shift?
Laura — can you approve the carveout path (we can use the SLA exception template attached) and confirm who signs off externally?
Marissa — please draft the customer communication (concise, no speculative cause).

I’ve attached the customer snapshot and the SLA exception template.

— Ben
Head of Sales, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com


From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Tim Zhao <tim.zhao@nimbushealth.com>
Date: 2028-06-11T10:02:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: sudden tail latency spike on phi7 (prod) — request for SLO carveout & traffic shift

Ben / team — quick tech note from runtime:
- We’re seeing increased KV cache misses on phi7 for long-sequence requests (profile matches customer graph). The kernel selection path for seq>1024 appears to be choosing the slower attention kernel under current flags.
- phi6 uses a different compilation profile (and a narrower attention path) — we’ve validated compatibility for the /generate route and a per-customer traffic shift is supported.

Estimate for safe traffic shift to phi6 for Nimbus only: ~25 minutes to stage, validate with canary at 5% then ramp to 100% (we’ll keep the KV cache cold-warm monitoring on). Rollback path: instant (repoint route back to phi7).

Requesting approval to execute the targeted traffic-shift now. From an ops perspective, this is low-risk and reversible; it should mitigate the p95 within the canary window.

Telemetry links: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/INF-9823 (runtime traces), and I’ll push a short dashboard to the Slack incident channel.

— Rishi
Director, Runtime Engineering
rishi.malhotra@redwood.com


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-06-11T11:15:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: sudden tail latency spike on phi7 (prod) — request for SLO carveout & traffic shift

Ben / Rishi — I approve the targeted traffic shift to phi6 for Nimbus Health and a temporary SLO carveout for this impacted scope. Conditions:
- Carveout window up to 48 hours unless we have a verified mitigation earlier.
- We must run the 5% canary and confirm p95 reduces before ramp.
- Any customer-facing messaging must reference a remediation window, not a root cause. If credits are requested, we’ll start with a standard 10% usage credit for the affected billing period and escalate if persistent.

Marissa: please use the SLA exception template and include the authorized window and the 10% credit placeholder. Copy legal if they request contract changes — but we should avoid legal until they push for permanent credits.

I’ll add a line to the exception doc: "Authorized by Laura Bennett (Product) — targeted traffic shift & 48h SLO carveout."

— Laura
VP Product, Redwood Inference
laura.bennett@redwood.com


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>

- We have an approved SLO carveout for this customer for up to 48 hours (Laura Bennett). We will monitor and update within that window.
- We’ve provisionally reserved a 10% usage credit for the affected billing period; we’ll confirm final credits once we validate mitigation or if the issue persists.

Next steps for internal ops: Rishi to run the shift and post canary metrics. Marissa to send the customer-facing note below (sending now):

Thanks for flagging the latency behavior. We’re initiating a targeted traffic shift to a validated variant while we investigate. Regards,

title: Close anchoring — MAP, procurement hold, and escrow option
preview:
From: Olivia Park <olivia.park@lumina.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@lumina.com>
Date: Thu, 7 Jun 2028 12:03:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Draft MAP and target close

Ben,

Thanks for the updated MAP earlier. Procurement surfaced a legal hold around data residency in their RFI that requires sign-off from Legal and our cloud team before we can commit to a firm close date. That likely shifts us ~3 weeks. I don't want to stall momentum — can we discuss options like an escrow arrangement for the initial model artifact or a staged acceptance? Attached the RFI (Procurement_RFI.pdf) for your team.

—Olivia



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Olivia Park <olivia.park@lumina.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 7 Jun 2028 12:31:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Draft MAP and target close

Olivia — thanks for the heads up and for the RFI.

Laura/Grace — FYI, Lumina's procurement put a legal/data residency hold into their RFI; they estimate ~3 week impact. We need to surface mitigation and an escalation plan. Options to propose to Lumina:

- Escrow for model artifacts + early staging access under a narrow NDA (reduces perceived vendor risk)
- Staged acceptance: sandbox + limited-scope go/no-go by feature set
- Price/term hold: guarantee current commercial terms for X weeks

Attaching MAP_v3 with a redlined contingency lane and proposed milestones (Lumina_MAP_v3.xlsx).

Olivia — would any of the above be acceptable as interim commitments while Legal completes their review?

Cheers,
Ben
Account Executive
Redwood Inference
attachments: Lumina_MAP_v3.xlsx (spreadsheet)



From: Olivia Park <olivia.park@lumina.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@lumina.com>, James Holloway <james@legalpartners.com>
Date: Fri, 8 Jun 2028 09:08:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Draft MAP and target close

Ben,

Thanks — staging access + price hold both sound promising. Legal (James) says escrow is viable but needs precise scope and trigger conditions. Procurement wants the MAP to reflect the hold window and a single-point SLA commitment for demo stability.

Proposed new target: provisional close 2028-06-28 (formal close pending legal sign-off). Procurement will aim to finish the RFI answers by 2028-06-20.

If Redwood can provide a sandbox with anonymized data sampling and a price hold through 2028-07-15, we can commit to the provisional timeline.

Olivia



From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 8 Jun 2028 10:37:00 -0700
Subject: internal: Lumina procurement hold — plan

Team — quick internal note before we reply to Lumina.

Actions I'm putting in motion:
1) Legal (me) will draft an escrow term sheet (conditions/escrow release) for James to review — due 2028-06-11 EOD.
2) Eng (Grace) to stand up a sandbox with an anonymized sample dataset and 2-week access token; include monitoring hooks for demo SLOs — ETA 2028-06-12.
3) Commercial team (Ben) to offer a price hold through 2028-07-15 and firm provisional close cadence.

We should accept their provisional close date but call out the MAP lane: 'provisional close' -> 'formal acceptance upon legal sign-off'. I'll own the finance/contract loop and loop in solutions enablement if needed.

Laura
Director, Legal Ops
Redwood Inference



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Olivia Park <olivia.park@lumina.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, James Holloway <james@legalpartners.com>, Procurement Team <procurement@lumina.com>
Date: Sat, 9 Jun 2028 16:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Draft MAP and target close

Olivia — thanks for the clear path forward. We're aligned on the provisional timeline and mitigations:

- Redwood will provide a sandbox with an anonymized sampling dataset and monitoring hooks by 2028-06-12 (Grace will run enablement).
- Redwood will hold pricing and current SOW terms through 2028-07-15.
- Laura will share an escrow term sheet by 2028-06-11 for legal review (James).
- Lumina to complete RFI and final legal sign-off by 2028-06-20.

We'll update the attached MAP to include a 'provisional close' lane and the trigger for 'formal acceptance' after legal sign-off. If all items complete on schedule, provisional close 2028-06-28 -> formal close by 2028-07-01. If legal requires material changes, we will preserve the price hold and revisit timeline.

Next step: Olivia — can you confirm the procurement team accepts the sandbox scope (anonymized, no PHI) and the 2-week staging window?

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/bencarter

title: Post-demo: NovaHealth POC — SDK onboarding + req/resp clarifications
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Jamie Liu <jamie.liu@novahealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 5 Jun 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Post-demo: NovaHealth POC — SDK onboarding + req/resp clarifications

Thanks again for taking the demo today — excited by the use case you walked through for automated discharge summaries + downstream coding/reranking. This thread is a short post-call recap and proposed next steps so we keep momentum. Key call takeaways: 

- POC goal: validate end-to-end throughput for nightly batch summarization + real-time triage in the app.
- Preferred infra: run Redwood Private in your AWS account (VPC peering) for PHI; initial scope limited to 2 GPU nodes and fallback to Hosted API for spikes.
- Security ask: NovaHealth will want SOC2 evidence + network diagram and an initial data retention policy.

Open technical items (we should confirm these before kick-off):
1) SDK / auth: do you prefer short-lived API keys with our CLI bootstrap, or a customer-managed OIDC federated token? If OIDC, we need issuer metadata and a service account flow.
2) Routing model: primary model for summaries is rdx-1.7 (quantized); fallback to rdx-1.1 if latency spikes. We should confirm acceptable latency bounds.
3) Structured output / tool calling: you mentioned native ICD-10 code lookup — we recommend Redwood tool-calls for deterministic code mapping. Are you OK returning JSON schema with strict fields (code, confidence, evidence_span)?
4) Embeddings & rerankers: confirm whether you want on-the-fly embeddings per record or a nightly batch index; this affects latency and cost.
5) Req/resp schema: team needs clarity on chat vs completions for the summarization flow (we recommended completions with structured JSON output and streaming enabled for the early UX).

Next steps I propose:
- Ben/Monica: share a one-page PoC plan + SOW draft (attached).
- Jamie: send the preferred auth approach (API key vs OIDC) and a contact on infra.
- NovaHealth: confirm retention max (30/90/365 days) for POC.

Attached: Redwood_POCPlan_v1.xlsx, SOW_draft_NovaHealth.pdf.

Cheers,
Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Solutions — Redwood Inference
[Attachment: Redwood_POCPlan_v1.xlsx (Excel, 45KB)]
[Attachment: SOW_draft_NovaHealth.pdf (PDF, 210KB)]

From: Jamie Liu <jamie.liu@novahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: infra@novahealth.com, procurement@novahealth.com
Date: Tue, 5 Jun 2028 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: NovaHealth POC — SDK onboarding + req/resp clarifications

Thanks Ben — great demo. Quick answers and a couple clarifying questions below.

Quick answers: 
- Auth: we prefer OIDC with short-lived tokens tied to a service account. I'll loop in our infra lead, Sam Park (infra@novahealth.com), to provide issuer metadata.
- Retention: want to start with 30 days for POC.
- Embeddings: prefer nightly batch index for full patient corpus to control costs.

Clarifying questions: 
1) For structured JSON output and tool-calls: does Redwood validate the schema server-side or do we validate client-side? We need strictness to avoid downstream parser errors.
2) Chat vs completions: our UI is conversational for clinical review. We need streaming semantics so reviewers can start reading partial output. But we also need deterministic stop behavior to ensure JSON output ends cleanly — how do you guarantee both?
3) Tokenization / max_tokens: some notes go to 8k tokens — do you recommend chunking on our side or using the runtime to handle windowing?

I'll have Sam share OIDC metadata by EOD tomorrow. Also attaching our initial security evidence request (security-evidence-list.xlsx).

—Jamie
Jamie Liu
Director, Clinical AI — NovaHealth
[Attachment: security-evidence-list.xlsx (Excel, 12KB)]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Jamie Liu <jamie.liu@novahealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 5 Jun 2028 13:28:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: NovaHealth POC — SDK onboarding + req/resp clarifications

Appreciate the quick replies and the evidence checklist — that's helpful. Answers inline below.

1) Schema validation: Redwood can do server-side enforcement as an optional strict mode. For POC we recommend strict=true for the summarization endpoint (so runtime rejects responses that violate the schema) and a mocked fallback that returns an error envelope when validation fails. We will surface validation failures to logs and the console.

2) Streaming + deterministic stops: you can enable streaming for partial reads while the runtime buffers tokens until it detects a schema-complete marker (we recommend a closing JSON sentinel like \"__END_JSON__\"). Implementation: the runtime emits chunks but includes a finalization event when the schema is complete; the SDK will reconstruct and validate before returning the final structured object. This keeps UX streaming while maintaining structural guarantees.

3) Tokenization & long notes: we recommend pre-chunking on the client if you need sentence-aware boundaries for coherent summaries. Redwood has an optional windowed request helper in the SDK that performs overlap-context stitching with cost controls. For 8k inputs, use chunking with overlap=256 tokens and our reranker to pick the most relevant contexts for a bounded call.

4) Embeddings flow: nightly batch index is fine. We'll provide an edge worker recipe for embedding generation and a reranker model that runs at query time. That approach minimizes real-time cost and keeps inference latency below 300ms for rerank lookups.

Proposed next steps (short timeline):
- Wed 6 Jun: Sam shares OIDC issuer metadata.
- Thu 7 Jun: Redwood shares a short-runbook (SDK bootstrap commands + sample config) and a whitelisted test endpoint for your infra team.
- Wed 13 Jun: Kick-off call with infra + security to review network diagram and SOC2 artifacts.

Marissa — can you own the SOW follow-up and procurement touchpoints? Monica — can you prepare the SDK bootstrap and windowed helper sample?

Ben
[Quoted message below]
> On Tue, Jun 5, 2028 at 09:12 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai> wrote:
> > Thanks again for taking the demo today — excited by the use case ...

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Jamie Liu <jamie.liu@novahealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, procurement@novahealth.com
Date: Wed, 6 Jun 2028 08:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: NovaHealth POC — SDK onboarding + req/resp clarifications

Jamie — looping in procurement to start the SOW/PO cadence. Ben — attaching a clean SOW draft with two optional commitment tracks (monthly reserved vs on-demand). Highlights:
- Milestones: SDK onboarding (week 1), PoC validation (week 3), handoff & knowledge transfer (week 5).
- Payment terms: standard 30 days net.
- SLA for POC: 99.5% uptime for the Private control plane; we include a three-day remediation clause.

Next ask for NovaHealth procurement: please confirm the billing entity and PO flow so we can attach to the SOW.

Thanks,
Marissa
Marissa Cole
Commercial Lead — Redwood Inference
[Attachment: SOW_draft_NovaHealth.pdf (PDF, 210KB)]

For 8k inputs, use chunking with overlap=256 tokens and our reranker to pick the most relevant contexts for a bounded call.

4) Embeddings flow: nightly batch index is fine. We'll provide an edge worker recipe for embedding generation and a reranker model that runs at query time. That approach minimizes real-time cost and keeps inference latency below 300ms for rerank lookups.

Proposed next steps (short timeline):
- Wed 6 Jun: Sam shares OIDC issuer metadata.
- Thu 7 Jun: Redwood shares a short-runbook (SDK bootstrap commands + sample config) and a whitelisted test endpoint for your infra team.
- Wed 13 Jun: Kick-off call with infra + security to review network diagram and SOC2 artifacts.

Marissa — can you own the SOW follow-up and procurement touchpoints? Monica — can you prepare the SDK bootstrap and windowed helper sample?

Ben
